title: First Coffee

compatibility.canDoAndObjectives: Both fields are retained because the frozen schema and current UI support them.
compatibility.achievementAndAchievementsUnlocked: The achievement object provides display metadata; achievementsUnlocked preserves stable global achievement IDs.
compatibility.legacyXpFields: Use rewards as the explicit source of truth; legacy xpReward fields remain as renderer fallbacks.

vocabulary.title: Words you’ll use today
vocabulary.body: Everything you need for your first meeting.

grammar.title: Say your name naturally
grammar.body: Learn one phrase you can use in almost every introduction.

dialogue.title: Your first café conversation
dialogue.body: Meet Carlos one simple line at a time.

listening.title: Can you follow along?
listening.body: Listen once. You don’t need to catch every word.

pronunciation.title: Speak like Carlos
pronunciation.body: Hear each phrase, then say it naturally.

speaking.title: Talk with Carlos
speaking.body: Listen, reply, and keep the conversation moving.

culture.title: A warmer hello
culture.body: One small habit that makes greetings feel natural in Madrid.

reward.title: You did it.
reward.body: Your first conversation is now part of your Spanish story.

story.scene: You meet Carlos at a neighborhood café for the first time.
story.mission: Today you’ll learn to greet Carlos, exchange names, and close your first Spanish conversation naturally.
story.continuity: This is the beginning of the Habla journey. Carlos will introduce you to his friend Ana next.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner is unsure what a first Spanish conversation will feel like.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner wants to discover who Carlos will introduce next.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Completing a warm first greeting from hello to goodbye.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Mañana quiero presentarte a mi amiga Ana.
microCliffhanger.english: Tomorrow you’ll meet my friend Ana outside the café. She speaks a little faster than I do, so get ready!

carlosIntroduction.text: ¡Hola! I’m Carlos. We’re meeting for coffee in Madrid today. By the end, you’ll introduce yourself, answer one simple question, and say goodbye naturally. Let’s go.

canDo: Greet someone naturally at different times of day.
Exchange names and say nice to meet you.
Answer how you are and return the question.
Ask for repetition when you do not understand.
End a first meeting with a natural goodbye.
objectives: Greet someone naturally at different times of day.
Exchange names and say nice to meet you.
Answer how you are and return the question.
Ask for repetition when you do not understand.
End a first meeting with a natural goodbye.

essentialPhrases.usage: A natural, widely useful way to begin a friendly conversation.
A clear introduction that works in formal and informal situations.
Say this after you exchange names.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: My name is Laura. Nice to meet you.

essentialPhrases.usage: A complete, friendly response that keeps the conversation moving.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: —How are you? —Very well, thanks. And you?

essentialPhrases.usage: Use this to repair the conversation when speech is too fast.
A polite survival phrase for anything you did not catch.
A natural closing when you expect to meet again.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Nice to meet you. See you later.

vocabulary.tip: The safest greeting in almost any situation.
Use from morning until around midday.
Use after midday and before evening.
Use as a greeting at night or as a goodbye before bed.
Use after learning someone's name.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Nice to meet you, my name is {{learnerName}}.

vocabulary.tip: Use encantado if you identify as male, encantada if female.
Informal; use with people your age, friends, and family.
Formal; use with strangers, elders, or in professional settings.
A short, common answer to ¿cómo estás?
A warmer answer than bien.
Use when you are okay but not great.
Informal way to return the question.
Formal way to return the question.
Literally means I call myself.
Use soy plus your name as a short, natural introduction.
Essential politeness word.
A warmer thank you.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Thank you very much for the help.

vocabulary.tip: The standard response to gracias.
Often goes at the end of a request.
Use for small interruptions or apologies.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, what is your name?

vocabulary.tip: Use when passing by or entering/leaving a space.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, I am going to pass.

vocabulary.tip: Use for apologies with more emotional weight.
Formal and useful with strangers.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, do you speak English?

vocabulary.tip: Use this as the lesson’s main friendly closing. Hasta luego is an equally natural alternative you may hear.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Nice to meet you. See you later.

vocabulary.tip: A very common alternative to nos vemos luego.
Useful, but it can sound more final than nos vemos luego.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Adiós. Que tengas un buen día.

greetings.whenUsed: Use hola everywhere. ¿Qué onda? is casual in Mexico; ¿qué tal? is very common in Spain and broadly understood.
Use in the morning. Mexican ¿cómo amaneciste? asks how someone woke up and feels warm or familiar.
Use after midday. Buenas is a shortened casual greeting common in many regions.
Use at night. It can be a greeting or a farewell.
Use with friends, peers, and family. ¿Qué tal? is shorter and very natural.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address respectfully.
Use immediately after introductions. Encantado/encantada changes with speaker gender.
Me llamo is clear and beginner-friendly. Soy is shorter and very natural in introductions.
Use after answering a greeting. Use usted instead of tú in formal situations.
Adiós is final. Nos vemos and hasta luego feel more like see you later.
Use when you expect to see the person the next day.
Casual only. Avoid with formal contacts or first meetings unless the tone is relaxed.
Use when receiving someone. Bienvenido changes to bienvenida for a woman.
Use formal conocerle/conocerlo with respect. Conocerte is informal.
Use after the first hello to sound more conversational.

grammar.meaning: Say your name in one clear, reusable phrase.
grammar.explanation: Learn “Me llamo…” as one complete phrase. You’ll use it almost every time you introduce yourself.

notes.title: The safest first phrase
notes.text: Me llamo {{learnerName}} means My name is {{learnerName}} and works in almost any introduction.

notes.title: A natural shorter option
notes.text: Soy Laura means I’m Laura. Soy also appears in the next lesson when you say where you are from.

examples.english: Hi, I’m Laura. Nice to meet you.

grammar.practicePrompt: Greet Carlos and say your real name with me llamo.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Good morning. My name is Carlos.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Nice to meet you, my name is {{learnerName}}.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Very well, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Excuse me, what is your name?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: My name is {{learnerName}}. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hi, I’m {{learnerName}}. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi, how's it going? How is everything?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Thank you very much, nice to meet you.

dialogue.title: Your first café conversation
dialogue.setting: Carlos arrives at a neighborhood café in Madrid and sits down across from you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi! My name is Carlos. What’s your name?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hi, my name is {{learnerName}}. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Nice to meet you, {{learnerName}}. How are you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Muy bien, gracias. Mi amiga Ana viene mañana. Quiero presentártela.
lines.english: Very well, thanks. My friend Ana is coming tomorrow. I want to introduce you to her.

comprehension.prompt: What is the learner’s first task?
How does the learner say they are doing?

supplementalPronunciation.focus: Clear vowels, silent h, Spanish ñ, and polite greeting rhythm.
tips: The h is silent in hola and hasta. Start with the vowel sound: ola, asta.
Keep Spanish vowels short and steady. Do not turn hola into hoh-luh.
The ñ in mañana sounds like ny in canyon: ma-NYA-na.
In most of Latin America, gracias uses an s sound: GRA-sias.
In much of Spain, gracias may use a th sound: GRA-thias.
In ¿cómo está?, stress the final tá. In ¿cómo estás?, stress tás.
For polite requests, slow down slightly: Perdón... ¿puede repetir?

items.note: Do not pronounce the h: ola.
Stress dí in días; keep buenos smooth.
Keep the final s light but audible.
Say both words clearly; do not drop buenas.
The u in gusto is pronounced: GUS-to.
Informal; keep the final s audible.
Pause slightly after más for clarity.

tips: The h is silent in hola and hasta.
Keep Spanish vowels short and clear.
Say greetings as relaxed phrases rather than isolated words.

items.note: Stress có in cómo and tás in estás.
The h is silent; use one relaxed closing rhythm.

listening.title: Can you follow our conversation?
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
listening.naturalScript: ¡Hola! ¿Qué tal? Me llamo Carlos. Mucho gusto. ¿Cómo te llamas? Mucho gusto, {{learnerName}}. ¿Cómo estás? Muy bien, gracias. ¿Y tú? Muy bien, gracias. Nos vemos luego.
listening.slowScript: ¡Hola! / ¿Qué tal? / Me llamo Carlos. / Mucho gusto. / ¿Cómo te llamas? / Mucho gusto, {{learnerName}}. / ¿Cómo estás? / Muy bien, gracias. / ¿Y tú? / Muy bien, gracias. / Nos vemos luego.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: ¡Hola! ¿Qué tal? Me llamo Carlos. Mucho gusto.
transcript.english: Hi! How’s it going? My name is Carlos. Nice to meet you.

transcript.speaker: Learner
transcript.english: My name is {{learnerName}}. Nice to meet you.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.english: Nice to meet you, {{learnerName}}. How are you?

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Muy bien, gracias. Nos vemos luego.
transcript.english: Very well, thanks. See you later.

comprehension.explanation: Carlos says “Me llamo Carlos” before asking the learner’s name.

comprehension.prompt: What does Carlos ask after introducing himself?
options: What the learner wants to drink

comprehension.prompt: How does the learner respond to ¿Cómo estás?
comprehension.explanation: The learner answers the check-in and naturally returns the question.
Nos vemos luego is a natural friendly way to say “See you later.”

listeningPhrases.english: Good morning, nice to meet you.

speaking.instructions: Carlos guides you through the café conversation one response at a time. Listen, answer, and keep the exchange moving.

items.label: Listen
items.prompt: Reply however you’d naturally greet him.
items.cue: Hear Carlos once, then greet him back.
items.focus: Keep both vowels in hola short and do not pronounce the h.

items.label: Your turn
items.cue: Say your own name and introduce yourself aloud.
items.meaning: My name is {{learnerName}}. Nice to meet you.
items.focus: Link me llamo smoothly and pause before mucho gusto.

items.label: Keep talking
items.cue: Answer naturally, then ask Carlos the same thing.
items.focus: Let your voice rise slightly on ¿Y tú?

items.label: One more challenge
items.cue: Try it from memory before playing the model.
items.focus: Stress más and pa in despacio.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.focus: Say the phrase as one relaxed closing.

miniConversation.setting: You meet Carlos at a café in Madrid.
miniConversation.goal: Exchange greetings and names, respond naturally, and close the conversation.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: Hi, how’s it going? My name is Carlos.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.exampleEnglish: Hi! Nice to meet you. My name is {{learnerName}}.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: Nice to meet you, {{learnerName}}. How are you?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Say how you are and return the question.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Muy bien, gracias. Bienvenido a Madrid.
turns.english: Very well, thanks. Welcome to Madrid.

culture.speaker: Carlos
culture.title: Greeting with warmth and respect
culture.text: When I meet someone here in Madrid, I usually say “Hola, ¿qué tal?” instead of only hola. After we exchange names, mucho gusto makes the greeting feel warm and natural.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers really greet
notes: Many speakers shorten buenas tardes or buenas noches to buenas in casual settings.
¿Qué tal? is one of the safest natural greetings across Spain and much of Latin America.
In Mexico, ¿qué onda? is friendly and casual, but it is not for formal introductions.
Native speakers often stack phrases: Hola, ¿qué tal? Mucho gusto.
Bien, gracias. ¿Y tú? is simple, polite, and useful when you are just starting.
With older relatives or professional contacts, ¿cómo está? sounds warmer and safer than ¿cómo estás?

commonMistakes.correction: Use ¿cómo está? in formal or respectful situations.
commonMistakes.explanation: The formal version is safer with strangers, elders, and people you have just met.

commonMistakes.mistake: Pronouncing the h in hola or hasta.
Using adiós when you expect to see someone soon.
commonMistakes.correction: Use hasta luego or nos vemos.
commonMistakes.explanation: Adiós can sound more final than see you later.
The fixed greeting is plural: buenos días.

commonMistakes.correction: Say bien, gracias. ¿Y tú? or ¿y usted?
commonMistakes.explanation: Returning the question makes the greeting feel complete and polite.

supplementalQuizBank.prompt: Choose the formal way to ask “How are you?”
Which phrase is the formal “And you?”
You are meeting your partner’s grandmother. Choose the safest greeting.
Choose the natural casual Mexican greeting.
Choose the natural Spain Spanish greeting for “How’s it going?”
Choose the Spanish for “My name is {{learnerName}}.”
A friend says: Hola, ¿qué tal? Choose a natural reply.
Someone speaks too fast. What should you say?

quiz.explanation: Hola is the safest all-purpose Spanish greeting.
Use buenos días during the morning.
Say mucho gusto after you learn someone’s name.

quiz.prompt: Choose the Spanish for My name is {{learnerName}}.
quiz.explanation: Use me llamo followed by your name.

quiz.prompt: Carlos says: Hola, ¿qué tal? Choose a natural reply.
quiz.explanation: Answer the check-in, then use ¿y tú? to return the question.
Buenas tardes is the standard afternoon greeting.

quiz.prompt: What should you say after Carlos tells you his name?
quiz.explanation: Mucho gusto naturally follows an exchange of names.

quiz.prompt: Someone is speaking too quickly. What should you say?
quiz.explanation: This politely asks the speaker to slow down.

quiz.prompt: You did not hear Carlos. Which phrase asks him to repeat?
Which phrase is a natural friendly closing?
quiz.explanation: Nos vemos luego means See you later.
The complete introduction pattern is me llamo + name.
¿Y tú? returns the same question informally.
Buenas noches works as both an evening greeting and a nighttime farewell.

quiz.prompt: Put the first meeting in the most natural order.
quiz.explanation: Begin with a greeting, give your name, then say nice to meet you.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks ¿Cómo estás? Choose the most complete response.
quiz.explanation: This answers Carlos and keeps the conversation moving.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Carlos has just introduced himself. Greet him, say your name, and say nice to meet you.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, Carlos. Me llamo {{learnerName}}. Mucho gusto.

speakingChallenge.prompt: You are meeting an older family member for the first time. Use a formal greeting and return the question politely.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenas tardes. Muy bien, gracias. ¿Y usted?

speakingChallenge.prompt: A friend your age says “Hola, ¿qué tal?” Answer naturally and ask “and you?”
Carlos speaks too quickly. Ask him politely to repeat more slowly.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Perdón, ¿puede repetir más despacio, por favor?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce yourself to your spouse’s family using one time-of-day greeting, your name, and one polite expression.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenas tardes, me llamo {{learnerName}}. Mucho gusto.

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Your First Meeting
realLifeMission.mission: Meet Carlos at a café. Greet him, exchange names, answer how you are, and end the conversation naturally.
successCriteria: Open with hola, buenos días, buenas tardes, or buenas noches.
Introduce yourself with me llamo or soy.
Use mucho gusto after Carlos introduces himself.
Answer ¿cómo estás? and return the question with ¿y tú?
Close with nos vemos or hasta luego.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: Fantastic work today. You greeted me, introduced yourself, and completed your first Spanish conversation. I’m proud of you.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the completed exchange first. Correct only one greeting or introduction error that affects meaning.
realLifeMission.handoff: Mañana quiero presentarte a mi amiga Ana.

canDo: I can greet Carlos and tell him my name.
I can answer how I am and ask the question back.
I can ask someone to repeat or speak more slowly.
I can close a first meeting naturally.
review.conversationReturn: When Carlos introduces Ana next, reuse hola, me llamo, mucho gusto, and ¿y tú? without treating them as a separate review.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only the phrases needed to open, sustain, repair, and close a first meeting appear in the core deck.

passportStamp.title: First Conversation
passportStamp.description: You met Carlos and completed your first Spanish conversation.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 1.

achievement.title: First Steps
achievement.description: Earn your first XP and begin your Spanish journey.
